era_context: The early Roman Empire under Augustus (r. 27 BCE – 14 CE) and his successor Tiberius (r. 14–37 CE). This is the height of the Pax Romana in its infancy. In China, the Western Han dynasty is in its final decades under Emperor Cheng (r. 33–7 BCE) and his successors; the empire faces internal instability and external pressure from the Xiongnu. In India, the Indo-Greek kingdoms are fragmenting; the Satavahana Empire dominates the Deccan. In Mesoamerica, the Classic Maya civilization is developing; in the Americas, Hopewell culture flourishes in North America.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; archaeological evidence sparse for this specific year)
Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (Germanic and Celtic regions; oral tradition only)
Oceania (no written records; archaeological dating imprecise)

events.title: Death of Augustus; Tiberius assumes sole power
events.description: Augustus, the first Roman Emperor, dies on 19 August 14 CE at Nola. Tiberius, his adopted son and stepson, assumes power without serious challenge, consolidating the Principate. The transition is relatively smooth, though Tiberius is unpopular with the Senate and public. This marks the end of the Augustan age and the beginning of the Julio-Claudian succession. The year 10 CE falls within Tiberius's early reign, a period of relative stability and military consolidation on the frontiers.
key_figures: Livia Drusilla (widow of Augustus, mother of Tiberius)
events.certainty_note: Augustus's death is precisely dated to 19 August 14 CE by multiple ancient sources. The year 10 CE falls within Tiberius's reign (14–37 CE), which is well-attested.
cross_references: Relates to broader Julio-Claudian succession; precedes Tiberius's reign of 23 years

events.title: Roman military operations on the Rhine frontier
events.description: Under Tiberius's command (as legate under Augustus, continuing into his reign), Roman legions conduct campaigns to consolidate control of the Rhine frontier and suppress Germanic tribes. The period 10–16 CE sees intensive military activity, including the campaigns of Germanicus (Tiberius's adopted son) in later years. In 10 CE specifically, Roman forces are engaged in maintaining order and preventing incursions across the Rhine.
events.certainty_note: Military activity on the Rhine during this period is well-attested, though specific events in 10 CE are not individually documented. The broader campaign context is confirmed.
cross_references: Precedes the Varian Disaster (9 CE) by one year; leads to Germanicus's campaigns (14–16 CE)

events.title: Han Dynasty instability; Emperor Cheng's reign
events.description: In China, Emperor Cheng of Han (r. 33–7 BCE) is deceased by 10 CE; the throne is held by Emperor Xuan's successors. The Western Han dynasty is in decline, facing internal corruption, eunuch influence, and external pressure from the Xiongnu. The year 10 CE falls within a period of political instability and the gradual erosion of imperial authority that will culminate in the Wang Mang usurpation (9 CE) and the transition to the Eastern Han (25 CE). Court factionalism and the power of the imperial harem are dominant features.
key_figures: Eunuchs and imperial consorts (names vary by source)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: The broad context of Han decline is well-documented. Specific events in 10 CE are not individually named in surviving sources; dating is approximate.
cross_references: Part of the broader Western Han decline (c. 100–9 BCE); precedes Wang Mang usurpation (9 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of specific events in 10 CE is limited by the nature of ancient sources. While Augustus's death in 14 CE is certain, the year 10 CE itself is not marked by any singular, universally attested event in surviving primary sources. Military campaigns on the Rhine are attested for the broader period but not specifically for 10 CE. The Han Dynasty context is similarly general; no specific event is recorded for this exact year in Chinese sources. Modern scholarship does not dispute the broad political context, but the granularity of year-by-year events for this period is inherently limited.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 10 CE is moderate but heavily weighted toward the Roman Empire and China, where written records are relatively abundant. Roman sources are primarily later compilations (Tacitus, Cassius Dio, Suetonius) written 80–200 years after the events, introducing potential bias toward court narratives and the perspective of the victors. Chinese sources (Han Shu, Shiji) are similarly retrospective. For other regions—India, Mesoamerica, Germanic tribes, Sub-Saharan Africa—contemporary documentation is minimal or absent. The surviving record reflects the literate, centralized states of the Mediterranean and East Asia; peripheral regions are underrepresented or invisible.

graph_edges.note: Augustus's death in 14 CE marks the formal end of the Augustan age; Tiberius's reign begins
Rhine frontier campaigns lead to the Varian Disaster in 9 CE
Han Dynasty instability is concurrent with Wang Mang's rise to power (9 CE)
